content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 10, 1993

W. E. "Bob" McClellan
McClellan Buick, Inc.
7675 University Avenue
La Mesa, CA 91941

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-400

Dear Mr. McClellan:

You have requested advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ as they
pertain to your obligations as a member of the El Cajon City
Council.

QUESTION

May you participate in the decision regarding a mobilehome
rent control ordinance, even though a number of residents in the
city's mobilehome parks are sources of income to you?

CONCLUSION

We do not have sufficient facts to ascertain if you may
participate in the decision regarding the mobilehome rent control
ordinance. As discussed below, you may participate if the
decision will not have a material financial effect on any of the
residents who are sources of income to you or if the "public
generally" exception applies.

FACTS

The City of El Cajon is currently reviewing the issue of
mobilehome rent control. Such review may culminate in the
adoption of a mobilehome rent control ordinance. A number of
residents in the city's mobilehome parks are sources of income to
you of more than $250 in the past twelve months. These
individuals have purchased either a car or services from your
business, McClellan Buick. You have stated that it is foreseeable
that the decision relating to the imposition or non-imposition of

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-93-400
Page 2


rent control will have a material financial effect on the
residents in the city's mobilehome parks.

There are twenty-six mobilehome parks in El Cajon, with a
total of approximately 2,000 mobilehome households. You have
thirteen sources of income who reside in seven different
mobilehome parks. One of your sources of income owns a mobilehome
park in El Cajon.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making, or using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or
has reason to know the official has a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate
family, or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dol-
lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(a)-(d).

Your business, McClellan Buick, is considered an economic
interest of yours pursuant to Section 87103(a). The mobilehome
park residents and the mobilehome park owner who have purchased
either goods or services from McClellan Buick in an amount of $250
or more within the past twelve months are sources of income to you
for purposes of Section 87103(c). Therefore, you may not make,
participate in making, or attempt to use your official position to
influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a

File No. A-93-400
Page 3


reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on either your business
or any of your sources of income.

Foreseeability

Generally, the effect of a decision is considered reasonably
foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will
occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect is only
a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) You have stated that it is
foreseeable that the residents of the mobilehome parks will be
financially affected by any decision relating to the imposition or
non-imposition of rent control.

Materiality

The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether the financial effect of a decision is material, depending
on the specific circumstances of each decision. One of your
sources of income is the owner of a mobilehome park. It is not
clear whether the owner is an individual or a business entity. If
the owner is a business entity, Regulation 18702 would apply to
determine the material financial effect of a decision on a
business entity indirectly affected by the decision. Regulation
18702.2(g) provides the dollar threshold amounts to determine
materiality for a relatively small business. Please review this
regulation, copy enclosed.

Where sources of income are individuals, Regulation 18702.6
provides:

The effect of a decision is material as to an
individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
official if any of the following applies:

(a) The decision will affect the
individual's income, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by $1,000 or more;
or

(b) The decision will affect the
individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under
Section 18702.3 or Section 18702.4.

A mobilehome is a tangible asset. If the decision regarding
the rent control ordinance will affect the source of income's
income, investments, assets or liabilities by $1,000 or more, the
effect is considered material under Regulation 18702.6(a) and you
may not participate in the decision.

File No. A-93-400
Page 4


     In addition, a mobilehome park resident generally has a lease
agreement and a leasehold interest in real property (Section
82033), unless the resident has a periodic tenancy of one month or
less (Regulation 18233).  Regulation 18702.4 (copy enclosed)
defines the circumstances under which a decision will have a
material financial effect on a leasehold interest in real
property.  If there will be a material financial effect on any of
the residents who are sources of income to you, you may not
participate in the mobilehome rent control ordinance decision.

Public Generally

     Even when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87100.)  Under newly adopted Regulation 18703 (copy
enclosed), the public generally exception applies if the
governmental decision affects an official's economic interests in
substantially the same manner and to affect the economic
interests of a significant segment of the public.

     We do not have enough facts to ascertain if a "significant
segment" of the public will be affected.  Therefore, please review
the objective standards set forth in the regulation to determine
if the public generally exception applies.  If the public generally exception
applies, you may participate in the decision regarding the
mobilehome rent control ordinance.

     If you still cannot determine whether you may participate
after applying your specific facts to the law described above,
please contact me and I will be glad to assist you further.


                                        Sincerely,


                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        By:  Jill Stecher
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:ak

Enclosures
